---
title: "MLOps"
pagetitle: "Skein Lite: MLOps"
execute:
  freeze: true
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import json
S = json.load(open("../evaluation/reports/site_stats.json"))
```

::: {.eyebrow}
Detect, notify, propose. A person promotes.
:::

Nothing that changes what Sara says to a shopper ships itself. The loop detects a signal, notifies a human, registers a candidate only when the signal warrants one, and produces shadow evidence. A person makes the last call. There is no auto-promote path in CI or the serving loop: the score-gated `make promote` is human-invoked, and serving never reads the registry stage, so no shopper-facing change happens without a person.

## The human-gated loop

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  D[Detect:<br/>5 drift monitors,<br/>CT triggers] --> N[Notify:<br/>one deduped<br/>GitHub issue]
  N -->|quality signal| REG[Register challenger:<br/>proposed in the registry]
  N -->|data signal| INV[Human investigates<br/>index or ingest]
  REG --> SH[Human runs<br/>shadow replay]
  SH --> H{Human decides}
  H -->|approve| PROD[Challenger becomes<br/>champion]
  H -->|reject| KEEP[Champion stays]
  classDef rej fill:#f8ecec,stroke:#b23a3a,color:#13191e;
  class KEEP,INV rej;
```

### 1. Detect

Five monitors (`mlops/drift.py`, `make drift`) read the traces every turn already writes: query-embedding distance, retrieval-score PSI, confidence PSI, grounding PSI, and the thumbs-down rate, with the PSI monitors stratified by language. A weekly Continuous Training cycle (`mlops/ct.py`, `.github/workflows/ct.yml`, `make ct`) adds two more triggers: the schedule, and enough new human-verified answers from the review queue.

### 2. Notify

A signal becomes one labelled GitHub issue (`mlops/notify.py`), deduped by label and title, so a recurring signal updates the same thread instead of spamming new ones. The body carries the drift signals, the signal-to-action decision, the registered candidate with its MLflow link, and the exact commands to run next. The thread is the page, the review surface, and the audit trail in one place. Without `gh` or a token it dry-runs, so notify never breaks a cycle.

### 3. Register only when warranted

Drift is a trigger for investigation, not automatic retraining. `mlops/ct.py` classifies the signal before anything is registered:

| Signal | Action |
|---|---|
| Confidence, grounding, or feedback drift, or enough new verified answers | Register a candidate prompt experiment as `proposed`, for human review |
| Query-embedding or retrieval-score drift | Notify only: an intent-mix or index problem a prompt cannot fix. A human investigates ingest and retrieval |
| Nothing over threshold | Quiet cycle, still recorded for the audit trail |

A candidate is registered only after it beats the baseline on a held-out split with the regression gate and the safety check green. Even then it is only `proposed`.

### 4. Shadow, run by a human

Before promoting, the reviewer runs `make shadow` (`mlops/shadow.py`). It replays the last N real shopper questions through the challenger, offline with zero user risk, and compares grounding, route, and cost against the champion's recorded results. The delta is the evidence a person approves with, logged to MLflow.

### 5. Promote, by a human

`make registry-promote V=<n>` archives the incumbent so exactly one config is ever live. When a tracking server is configured, `make promote` mirrors the transition into the real MLflow Model Registry with the measured RAGAS score attached: 0.65 for staging, 0.78 for production, a production candidate must beat the champion, and it refuses outright if no real score was ever measured for this exact config.

## The model registry

Every artifact this system owns and versions, a prompt candidate or a serving config, moves through staged versions (`mlops/model_registry.py`, `make registry`):

```
proposed (@challenger)  ->  staging  ->  production (@champion)
```

MLflow deprecated stages in favour of aliases, so the registry speaks that vocabulary: the one `production` version is the `@champion`, the latest `proposed` candidate is the `@challenger` a human is deciding on, and the stage is kept as the `validation_status` tag. Every CT run is recorded, promotable or not, so "registered on a weekly cadence" is true and reviewable. The store is JSON-backed so it works offline in CI and the demo.

## Four MLflow experiments

Each stage of the loop logs to its own experiment, so a decision is a run you can open, not a screenshot:

| Experiment | What lands there |
|---|---|
| `skein-drift` | every drift run: the five monitor scores and which tripped |
| `skein-ct` | each CT cycle: trigger, baseline vs candidate, the registered version |
| `skein-shadow` | champion vs challenger deltas per replay |
| `skein-faithfulness` | the online judge's sampled scores from live traffic |

The demo defaults to a local `./mlruns` file store; set `MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI` for a server and everything logs there unchanged.

## Four monitoring pillars

One trace store feeds four questions, so a bad answer is explained, not guessed at:

| Pillar | Question | Watched | Where |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Has the input shifted? | the five drift monitors | `make drift`, `skein-drift` |
| **Model** | Is the answer still good? | RAGAS scores, routing accuracy, the eval gate, the faithfulness ladder | `make ragas`, `make gate` |
| **System** | Is it fast, and up? | p95 latency, error rate, cost per turn, throughput | `/api/admin/health` |
| **Business** | Is it earning its keep? | containment vs escalation, answer rate, thumbs, $ per turn and session | `/api/admin/business` |

The admin dashboards at `/admin` render the same traces as quality, health, business, and unmet-demand views for an operator.

## The data lifecycle is human-triggered too

The same propose-then-approve rule governs what Sara knows:

| Job | Proposes | The human step |
|---|---|---|
| Consolidation (`make consolidate`, `mlops/consolidate.py`) | a knowledge pack distilled from answers an operator already verified; repeat questions and abstain clusters surface as gaps to fill, never auto-answered | edit and approve; only then does the flywheel index it |
| Staleness (`mlops/staleness.py`) | a refresh of verified chunks older than the threshold | approve; nothing expires or re-indexes on its own |

Only human-verified content enters the index, and an LLM never rewrites stored knowledge, which is what keeps a self-improving corpus from corrupting itself.

## CI, the other loop

CI asks a different question on every push: is the code correct? Four jobs, and nothing merges unless all pass.

| Job | What it proves |
|---|---|
| **check** | lint, the `{python} S["tests"]`-test suite, domain validation, the leak linter, the offline eval gate |
| **dbt** | the medallion builds, governance tests pass, gold parity holds |
| **web** | the storefront lints and builds |
| **security** | Python and web dependency audits |

The leak linter backs the domain-swap claim: the build fails if a single product name, brand, or metric appears in engine code.

## What lives elsewhere

- The graded golden set that gates a model flip, and the quality thresholds behind promotion: [Evaluation](evaluation.qmd).
- The OPRO prompt loop the CT cycle re-runs (propose automatically, promote through a human): [Evaluation](evaluation.qmd#the-eval-as-a-target).
- What a turn and a session cost, and the derivation: [Cost](cost.qmd).
- Why the default path has no auto-promote, no framework brain (LangGraph is an opt-in lane, not the default), and no in-loop critic: [Decisions](decisions.qmd).
